current_fact.fact_text: In 2000s post‑conflict Kosovo, the 2004 riots showed how quickly tensions could reignite even under international supervision. The destruction of 35 Serbian Orthodox shrines and two mosques in Serbia underlined that in the Balkans, architecture can be both memory and target.

episode.title: Morning Drops #22 - Sicilians On The Dolomites
episode.description: Hosted by Yasmin, a musical journey inspired by the mountains. Joined by two special guests, exploring the tracks they've chosen, hearing the stories behind their picks, and discovering the personal memories each song brings to life — a blend of music, voices, and landscapes you can hear.
songs: Florian Pellissier Quintet - Coup de foudre à Thessalonique
Lucio Dalla & Francesco De Gregori - Ma come fanno i marinai

current_fact.match_reason: The violent targeting of historic shrines is a dark mirror to an episode where landscapes, mountains, and shared stories become sources of connection rather than division.

queue.fact_text: Across the centuries, March 17 has carried the weight of empire and rebellion—from Julius Caesar’s final battlefield triumph to Commodus’s solo rule, to Napoleon remaking Italy in his image. It’s a date that keeps returning to questions of power: who holds it, how it is claimed, and what happens when a single person stands at the center of a world.

episode.title: Morning Drops #44 - Residente
episode.description:
In this edition of Morning Drops, Yasmin navigates the complex geography of René Pérez Joglar, known to the world as Residente. His story is not just a musical evolution; it is a timeline marked by resistance, lyrical obsession, and a relentless search for identity.

From the gated streets of Trujillo Alto to the global stage, we explore how a restless mind transformed into the voice of a generation. The selection moves through the textures of his life: hearing the raw energy of his early rebellion, the sharp precision of his political critique, and finally, the fragile silence of his most personal confessions.

It is a study in duality. We witness the rapper who dismantles giants and the son who misses his mother. It is a soundscape that proves that sometimes, the loudest revolution happens inside.

Mood: Raw, Revolutionary, Introspective, Honest
Music genre: Latin Alternative, Hip Hop, Urban, World

queue.match_reason: This meta‑fact threads together imperial power stories on March 17 and pairs them with Residente, an artist obsessed with dismantling giants and interrogating authority through lyrics.

queue.fact_text: If you trace March 17 through time, you see a strange constellation: a solar‑powered satellite, a deadly nerve‑gas test, erupting volcanoes, collapsing tunnels, and voters ending apartheid. It’s a reminder that a single date on Earth can hold both quiet breakthroughs and loud catastrophes, all orbiting the same sun.

episode.title: Evening Flows #01 - Yasmin - Crescendo in the Medina
episode.description: The first Evening Flow episode comes from Yasmin. It starts deep in the medina, slow, mysterious, textured, and grows into a liberating dance across sub-Saharan landscapes. A journey through ambient soundscapes, Arabic melodies, Afro-Latin rhythms, and funky highlife grooves.
songs: David Visan & Carlos Campos - Crystal Heart
Evan Mars - Ya Zareef Al Tool
Dele Sosimi & Medlar - You No Fit Touch Am (Medlar Remix)

queue.match_reason: A closing, big‑picture reflection on March 17 suits an Evening Flow mix that slowly builds from mystery into movement, weaving many places and moods into a single luminous arc.

published.fact_text: In 1958, the tiny satellite Vanguard 1 became the first spacecraft to use solar cells as its primary power source, proving that sunlight could keep electronics alive in space. It’s still orbiting Earth today, making it the oldest human‑made object in space and a drifting time capsule of early spaceflight.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A groundbreaking space-technology milestone pairs naturally with an episode themed around reimagining 'space' as possibility and forward motion.

published.fact_text: In 1950, researchers at the University of California, Berkeley announced the creation of californium, element 98 on the periodic table. This intensely radioactive metal would later be used to start nuclear reactors, search for oil, and even help treat certain cancers, all from an element that weighs mere micrograms in most labs.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

published.match_reason: The discovery of a new element embodies scientific curiosity and persistence, echoing the episode’s focus on learning, experimentation, and delayed rewards.

published.fact_text: On March 17, 1966, the deep‑sea submersible Alvin located a missing American hydrogen bomb off the coast of Spain after a midair collision had dropped it into the Mediterranean. The search pushed the limits of underwater technology, turning Alvin into a legendary explorer of Earth’s hidden depths.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

published.match_reason: A deep‑ocean search for a hidden bomb is a literal immersion below the surface, resonating with an episode built around diving, water, and going deeper into experience.

published.fact_text: In 1968, nerve gas tests at Utah’s remote Skull Valley went catastrophically wrong when a drifting cloud of VX agent killed more than 6,000 sheep. The eerie sight of a silent valley filled with dead animals became a turning point in public awareness about chemical weapons and environmental risk.

episode.title: Morning Drops #52- Think Again
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the quiet art of changing your mind, guided by Adam Grant's book Think Again. From the beliefs we carry without questioning to the identities we build around what we think we know, this episode follows the slow, sometimes uncomfortable process of unlearning.

Along the way, we meet a Serbian trumpet player who had to let go of one dream to find another, a Tunisian oud master who challenged centuries of tradition from the inside, and a Japanese band who found freedom in playing for no one. The music moves from Balkan brass to Mediterranean stillness to psychedelic drift, tracing the shape of a mind opening up.

Listen slowly, and ask yourself: is there something you believe today that deserves a second look?

Tracklist:
Boban Marković Orkestar – SAT
Anouar Brahem – The Astounding Eyes of Rita
Kikagaku Moyo – Dripping Sun
Son Lux – Lost It to Trying

published.match_reason: A tragic experiment at the edge of ethics and ecology invites us to 'think again' about weapons, testing, and unintended consequences, mirroring the episode’s theme of unlearning and reassessment.

published.fact_text: In 1963, Bali’s sacred volcano Mount Agung erupted with devastating force, sending ash 10 kilometers into the sky and killing more than 1,100 people. Many Balinese interpreted the eruption as a spiritual sign, as it occurred near the time of major religious ceremonies on the island.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

published.match_reason: Both the volcanic eruption and the orca dives are encounters with overwhelming natural forces that inspire awe, fear, and spiritual reflection, fitting the episode’s cinematic, nature‑immersed mood.

published.fact_text: On March 17, 1862, Finland’s first railway line, the Päärata, officially opened between Helsinki and Hämeenlinna. This single track stitched together forests, lakes, and distant towns, quietly reshaping how people, ideas, and goods moved through the far north.

episode.title: Sedna FM #05 - On The Go - Zero Zero
episode.description: An energetic mix for when you're on the move. Zero Zero captures that feeling of starting fresh, moving forward, and embracing the journey. Perfect for travel, walks, or just getting things done.
songs: Grace Jones - Pull up to the bumper
Easy star all star - Soul love
James Brown - Talking loud and saying nothing (remix)

published.match_reason: The inauguration of a nation’s first railway is literally a story of being 'on the go,' matching an upbeat travel‑oriented mix about movement and new journeys.

published.fact_text: In 1979, Scotland’s Penmanshiel Tunnel collapsed during engineering works, killing two workers and burying part of a major railway link. Instead of reopening the tunnel, engineers ultimately rerouted the line over the hill, leaving the sealed portal as a quiet monument to the risks hidden inside the landscape.

episode.title: Morning Drops #28 - Under Scottish Skies
episode.description: Gaia shares a personal moment of transition — a shift in seasons, in rhythm, and in life. From this space of quiet change comes a musical journey through Scotland: not the usual names, but voices that speak from the edges. A personal ritual and a small act of curiosity: an invitation to notice what grounds us when everything moves.
songs: Paolo Nutini - Pencil Full of Lead

published.match_reason: A dramatic event in the Scottish rail network sits naturally under an episode that wanders musically ‘under Scottish skies,’ tying geography, memory, and infrastructure together.

published.fact_text: Also in 1979, Aeroflot Flight 1691 crashed on approach to Moscow’s Vnukovo International Airport after an in‑flight fire and cockpit confusion, killing 58 people. The accident highlighted how small errors in communication and decision‑making can cascade into catastrophe within minutes.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.

Mood: Reflective, Resilient, Grounded
Music genre: Indie Pop, Cinematic, World, Soul

We invite you to listen deeply and find comfort in your own pace.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

published.match_reason: A tragic crash and its aftermath call for a slower, reflective tone about fragility and recovery, parallel to an episode that meditates on injury, healing, and looking back.

published.fact_text: On March 17, 1957, a plane crash on Mount Manunggal in Cebu killed Philippine president Ramon Magsaysay and 24 others, abruptly ending the term of a leader widely seen as close to ordinary people. The hillside where the aircraft struck has since become both a memorial site and a pilgrimage destination.

episode.title: Morning Drops #36 - Women of Rock
episode.description: A tribute to the women who shaped the sound of rock, the ones who picked up a guitar and refused to ask for permission. The conversation unfolds in Italian, led by Gaia and her father. A dialogue between generations and a reminder that the pulse of rock has always belonged to anyone brave enough to play it loud. Distortion, rhythm, freedom.
songs: Joan Jett & The Blackhearts - I Love Rock 'n Roll

published.match_reason: The sudden loss of a national leader evokes themes of courage, legacy, and voices that lead; an episode about trailblazing women in rock mirrors that spirit of stepping forward and refusing to ask permission.

published.fact_text: In 1988, Avianca Flight 410, a Colombian Boeing 727, crashed into a fog‑shrouded mountain shortly after takeoff, killing all 143 people on board. The tragedy spurred changes in crew training and navigation procedures, turning a disaster into hard‑won lessons for safer skies.

episode.title: Morning Drops #25 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

published.match_reason: The somber, atmospheric textures of trip‑hop align with the reflective mood surrounding a mountain air disaster and the slow, systemic changes that followed.

published.fact_text: On March 17, 1948, Belgium, France, Luxembourg, the Netherlands, and the United Kingdom signed the Treaty of Brussels, a mutual defense pact born from the ruins of World War II. It became a key stepping stone toward NATO and the broader idea that security could be shared across borders.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

published.match_reason: A treaty that becomes the origin of a vast security alliance mirrors an inaugural episode that marks 'where it all began' for Sedna FM, both setting the foundations for what comes next.

published.fact_text: In 1960, U.S. President Dwight D. Eisenhower approved a covert program against Cuba that would eventually culminate in the Bay of Pigs invasion. What began as a classified directive would spill into one of the Cold War’s most infamous failed operations.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: A secretive anti‑Cuban program and its political fallout connect with an episode centered on Rage Against the Machine, whose music directly confronts U.S. foreign policy, power, and covert violence.

published.fact_text: On March 17, 1805, Napoleon transformed the Italian Republic into the Kingdom of Italy and crowned himself its king, extending his web of power across the Alps. The move layered a new royal title onto his already complex identity as general, consul, emperor, and self‑made legend.

episode.title: Sedna FM #06 - Alters of an Ego
episode.description: What if you could step outside yourself and become someone freer, bolder, or completely unexpected? Exploring the artistry of musicians who craft alter-egos and parallel realities to experiment with new dimensions of their identity. It's not about hiding who they are — it's about expanding who they can be.
songs: The Fireman - Strawberries Oceans Ships Forest

published.match_reason: Napoleon’s self‑reinvention from president to king to emperor echoes the episode’s exploration of alter egos and expanding identities, swapping stage names for grand titles.

published.fact_text: In 180, the 18‑year‑old Commodus became sole emperor of Rome after the death of his father, the philosopher‑emperor Marcus Aurelius. His erratic rule and love of gladiatorial spectacle helped steer the empire from stoic wisdom toward chaos and performance.

episode.title: Morning Drops #10 - The One With Many Names
episode.description: Yasmin guides us through the glamorous world of Ghost, the Swedish band that turned rock into ritual. From masked clergy to shifting identities, diving into the music and myth of one of the most theatrical acts of our time.

published.match_reason: Commodus’s theatrical, ritual‑heavy reign fits with an episode about Ghost, a band that turns power, religion, and identity into elaborate rock theater.

published.fact_text: On March 17, 44 BCE (45 by Roman counting), Julius Caesar won his final victory at the Battle of Munda, defeating the last major Pompeian forces in Spain. It secured his dominance over Rome—but also intensified fears that one man now overshadowed the republic itself.

episode.title: Morning Drops #37 - Happy Birthday Gaia
episode.description: A special Morning Drop to celebrate Gaia's birthday and the first anniversary of Sedna FM. A journey through friendship, music, and voice messages from the people who've known her longest. From street dance classes in Milan to creative adventures in London, a tribute to growing up together and showing up for each other.
songs: The Mighty Tiny & The Many Few - Good Foot

published.match_reason: Caesar’s last great victory marks a pivotal anniversary in Roman history; pairing it with a birthday/anniversary episode plays on the idea of dates that change everything, for better or worse.

published.fact_text: On March 17, 1776, the British army evacuated Boston after George Washington positioned captured artillery on the hills overlooking the city. The peaceful withdrawal ended the Siege of Boston and gave the American Revolution one of its first major strategic successes without a full‑scale battle.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: The end of the Siege of Boston is a shift from storm to clearer skies in a revolution; it aligns with an episode about transitions, hope after difficulty, and the sense that 'it can't rain forever.'

published.fact_text: In 1988, at the Battle of Afabet, Eritrean independence fighters launched a coordinated attack on the Ethiopian army’s Nadew Command, encircling it from three sides. The surprise victory shattered one of Ethiopia’s strongest formations and marked a turning point in the long Eritrean War of Independence.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

published.match_reason: A desert war for independence resonates with an episode about desert‑born music, exile, and resistance, even though it centers different communities; both channel the politics and poetry of arid lands.

published.fact_text: On March 17, 1992, white South Africans voted in a referendum to end apartheid, with nearly 69% supporting reforms that would dismantle the legal framework of racial segregation. It was a rare moment when a ballot box opened the door to a new constitutional order.

episode.title: Morning Drops #24 - The Rise Of Afrobeat
episode.description: An exploration of the rise of Afrobeat, tracing its roots and evolution as a genre that blends African rhythms with jazz, funk, and highlife. A celebration of the infectious energy and cultural significance of this influential musical movement.

published.match_reason: An anti‑apartheid referendum and an episode on Afrobeat—long a soundtrack of African political resistance—both celebrate liberation movements on the continent through different lenses.

published.fact_text: In 2004, ethnic unrest in Kosovo erupted into two days of violence that left more than 22 people dead and over 200 wounded, while dozens of Serbian Orthodox churches and mosques were destroyed. The clashes revealed how fragile peace can be when history, identity, and sacred places collide.

episode.title: Morning Drops #38 - History of Flamenco
episode.description: A special journey into the history of Flamenco, guided by Yasmin and her guest Almudena from Madrid, Spain. Exploring the roots and evolution of this emotionally raw and culturally rich tradition, from its early vocal origins and Andalusian influences, through its transformation on stage and screen, to its modern reinventions by iconic Spanish artists.
songs: La Niña de los Peines - Al Gurugú
Paco de Lucía - Entre dos aguas
Camarón de la Isla - La leyenda del tiempo

published.match_reason: Violence around churches and mosques in the Balkans sits in tension with an episode about another deeply charged cultural tradition—Flamenco—highlighting how art and identity can be sources of both division and healing.

published.fact_text: On March 17, 2000, more than 500 members of the Ugandan Movement for the Restoration of the Ten Commandments of God died in a church fire, with hundreds more later found in mass graves. What had looked like an apocalyptic religious community revealed itself as one of the deadliest cult tragedies in modern history.

episode.title: Morning Drops #13 - Demystifying Reggaeton
episode.description: Yasmin takes us on a journey through the real story of reggaeton — from its roots in Panama and Puerto Rico to its global evolution. Exploring the genre's history, influences, and cultural impact.
songs: Renato - Chica de los Ojos Café
B. F. Yaviah - Si Tu Quieres Fumar

published.match_reason: A cult that manipulated belief and narrative contrasts with an episode that 'demystifies' a misunderstood genre; both examine how stories are told, twisted, and reclaimed in culture.

published.fact_text: In 1988, the same year as the Afabet offensive and Avianca 410, the Ethiopian–Eritrean front was in flux, but far away in Colombia and Venezuela, a Boeing 727 hit a mountain in low visibility. 1988 became a year marked by conflict and catastrophe across continents, from highland battlefields to cloud‑covered peaks.

episode.title: Morning Drops #12 - 80's Club
episode.description: Starting with a Monday evening in Munich — a few drinks, friends, and landing in an 80s dance party. From Donna Summer's hypnotic pulse, to Depeche Mode's bright synthpop, Ken Laszlo's Italo Disco romance, and Mylène Farmer's darker theatrical world. Four songs, four corners of the 80s — connected by the machines that changed how we dance.
songs: Depeche Mode - Just Can't Get Enough

published.match_reason: By zooming out to the shared year 1988, we can contrast its heavy political and aviation disasters with an episode that celebrates the escapist, machine‑driven joy of 1980s club music worldwide.

episode.description: Hosted by Yasmin, a musical journey inspired by the mountains.
